Every time a user uses ChatGPT, every interaction becomes Azure's revenue. Now, switching costs here are high. It is not only a contract, but it's a matter of architecture because it is hard to reroute ChatGPT off Azure, and this agreement extends through 2032.

Finally, it is true that OpenAI is also diversifying its infrastructure risk. It signed a contract with AWS for its enterprise platform Frontier, for example. But Microsoft has willingly given up its compute exclusivity agreement with OpenAI to secure the API layer. We will see whether this strategy will pay off by reading Microsoft's cloud revenue growth trajectory.

So, Microsoft has a layered position when it comes to OpenAI: an equity stake, API exclusivity, and RPO. Bears believe that this will become a liability. Bulls think otherwise. But I believe that we should stay in between. Therefore, when it comes to our DCF, we could add a risk premium for the RPO, which is quite different from booking an impairment. Just to give you an idea, imagine that OpenAI's $280B commitment converts at a $0.80:$1.00 ratio, meaning that for every dollar of commitment, the real revenue becomes $0.80. My base case scenario for Microsoft moves from a fair value of $465 to $440. This is a $25 per share reduction. Considering that Microsoft is trading at $400, the market is actually implying a worse ratio.

Valuation

Remember, if we get a discount rate or a growth wrong, a DCF can give very different outputs. This is why I anchor my numbers to one of the best academics. As you know, when I run my DCF models, I follow Professor Damodaran's methodology. So I start with the risk-free ratio of the 10-yr Treasury, which is currently around 4.5%. This leads us to an equity risk premium of 4.8%. Using the software sector beat and Microsoft's debt-to-equity ratio (around 0.15 right now), we have a WACC of 9.5%. The terminal growth rate I am using is 3.5%, which is very moderate. After all, the nominal GDP growth rate is around 2.5%, which means that I am adding just 1 full percentage point as the premium. In my base scenario, I am also assuming that the operating margin for this year will be 45%, which is consistent with MSFT's guidance. I will also assume that they will gradually expand as the infrastructure buildout cycle moves closer to its maturity and we see the impact of operating leverage.

Scenario Revenue CAGR FY26-30 Margin WACC Fair Value
Bear 11% 44% 10.5% $340
Base 13% 46% 9.5% $465
Bull 15% 48% 8.5% $610

If you like a sensitivity table, below you can see the different outcomes of a WACC-terminal growth rate matrix.

WACC TGR 3% TGR 3.5% TGR 4%
8.5% $535 $565 $600
9.5% $430 $465 $505
10.5% $320 $340 $365

I consider the base case as the justified scenario right now. A bit of risk is present, but we should not overlook that we are talking about Microsoft. The bear case seems to me too unlikely, as it would need sharp margin compression and an RPO conversion disaster. The bull case is also a bit hard to predict, as the unknown variable is the ROI on capex.

At $400, Microsoft is a buy, and I am adding to my position as long as the stock stays between $380 and $410.

data.title: Service Properties Trust: Debt Crisis Overshadows Strategic Pivot
data.description:
Introduction

I last wrote about Service Properties Trust (SVC) a couple of months ago. I rated the company as a “buy,” stating that the company was in the midst of transforming itself from a hotel-focused REIT to a net lease REIT and the market was undervaluing the company, which made it a compelling investment opportunity.

Since I wrote about the company, its share price has increased by almost 18%, from $1.78 to $2.10. However, the company also released its Q4 2025 earnings during this period, which has led me to re-evaluate the company.

Quick Recap

The company’s portfolio is in 2 distinct categories—hotels and net lease properties. As of 31 December 2025, its portfolio comprises 760 net lease properties and 94 hotels. The company is in the middle of transforming to a predominantly net lease REIT. To that end, the company has been aggressively disposing of its hotels, which has continued into 2026. Since the start of the year, the company has disposed of 1 hotel, with a further 16 in the pipeline. The proceeds were used to pay off its existing debt, and the company has virtually no debt due in 2026.

2025 Results

For Q4 2025, Services Properties Trust reported revenue of $397.45 million and Normalized Funds From Operations (FFO) of $0.17/share, both of which exceeded expectations. Adjusted EBITDAre declined slightly to $550.3 million, while rental income remained flat at approximately $400 million. However, normalized FFO for FY 2025 was $0.78/share, a 28% decline from the $1.07/share in FY 2024.

However, looking ahead to the 2026 guidance paints a worrying picture. The company is expected to perform worse in virtually all metrics for 2026. Adjusted EBITDAre is expected to fall to between $500 million and $520 million, while even the high end of guidance for rental income will still see a fall to $386 million. Crucially, normalized FFO for FY 2026 is expected to decline to between $0.65/share and $0.77/share. In other words, the best-case scenario is a slight decrease, while the worst-case scenario is a further 17% drop in normalized FFO in 2026, on top of the 28% fall in 2025, essentially a 40% decline in 2 years.

Balance Sheet

As of 31 December 2025, total debt stands at $5.2 billion, compared to equity of $646 million, translating to a debt-to-equity ratio of around 8x. While the company has no maturities due this year, approximately 40% of its debt (around $2 billion) is due by 2028.

In February 2026, S&P Global downgraded the company’s credit rating to B- from B, stating that its outlook on the company remained negative over the view that "the company’s capital structure could become unsustainable over the next 12 months."

To partially address its upcoming maturities, the company priced $745 million in net lease mortgage notes recently, with a weighted-average coupon rate of 5.96%. The proceeds would be used to redeem $700 million in 8.375% senior guaranteed unsecured notes due in 2029 and is expected to save the company $14 million in annual interest expense. However, it doesn’t address the maturities due in 2027 and 2028, and further encumbers a substantial portion of the company’s net lease portfolio, thereby reducing future financing flexibility.

To make things worse, the company’s revolving credit facility matures in June 2027, with 2 six-month extension options available to the company for a final maturity of June 2028. With the downgrade in credit rating, would mean that a new revolver would come at higher interest rates.

Valuation

Taking the midpoint of the 2026 Adjusted EBITDAre guidance of $510 million and a 10x trading multiple will give the company approximately $5.1 billion in enterprise value. After deducting roughly $4.9 billion in net debt, the implied equity value is approximately $200 million and a share price of around $1.20/share, a 40% downside from the current price.

There are a couple of things to note about this valuation. First, the consensus analyst target paints a rosier picture with an average price target of $2.25/share and a “Hold” rating. However, the latest rating came in January 2026, before the company released its Q4 2025 earnings and 2026 guidance.

Also, a 10x trading multiple treats the company as a hotel REIT instead of a net lease REIT, which typically trades at around 16x. Even taking a midpoint 13x multiple would see the share price of the company valued at around $10/share. However, given the ongoing uncertainty over its balance sheet, I feel that a more conservative multiple is warranted.

Potential Counterarguments

Of course, there are potential scenarios whereby the company’s situation can improve. Firstly, the 2026 FIFA World Cup represents a near-term catalyst, with the company poised to benefit from increased numbers in June and July 2026.

Next, the company’s capital expenditures are declining, with projected capital expenditure for 2026 coming in at between $120 million and $140 million, down from over $300 million in both 2024 and 2025. This would positively impact the company’s free cash flow, though it remains to be seen if any impact will be meaningful in the short-term.

Finally, the best case scenario would be if the company is able to successfully extend or refinance its 2027 debt. This would reduce near-term default risk and also potentially spark a rally in the company’s share prices. Being able to refinance its near-term debt would give the company more breathing room and flexibility to manage its finances.

Conclusion

A lot is dependent on the company’s ability to meet its debt obligations over the next couple of years. The company has been able to meet some of its obligations through either selling its hotel portfolio or borrowing against its net lease portfolio. However, the number of hotels it has is not infinite, and the options available to the company shrink each time.

For investors, the risk-reward ratio doesn’t make sense. Despite the positives of the company’s strategic transformation to a net lease REIT, its looming substantial debt means that a lot of the value accrues to its creditors rather than shareholders. A poor 2026 forecast also doesn’t inspire confidence in the company’s ability to turn things around.

Moreover, as mentioned at the start of the article, the company’s share price has already increased by 18% since my last article, which has reduced some of the upside. I am rating the company as a “Sell” at the moment, though I will be monitoring to see how the company refinances its near-term debt before evaluating the company again.
